perturbation.original_text: The Parties irrevocably agree that the C ourts o f Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: The Parties irrevocably agree that the Courts of Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with the definition of 'Business Day' within this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By limiting the Delaware courts' jurisdiction to disputes arising from the definition of 'Business Day,' the clause contradicts the standard practice of Delaware law, which grants jurisdiction over the entire contract, not just a specific definition. This undermines the intended legal recourse for breaches of the broader agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law allows parties to agree to jurisdiction and venue, but limiting jurisdiction to a single definition within the agreement can be seen as an attempt to circumvent broader jurisdictional principles, potentially invalidating the entire jurisdiction clause if challenged.

perturbation.justification: The original text grants Delaware courts exclusive jurisdiction over any dispute arising from the agreement. The changed text narrows this jurisdiction to only disputes concerning the definition of 'Business Day.' This limitation contradicts the standard practice of Delaware law by restricting the court's ability to address broader contractual breaches. While Snippet 1 defines terms related to equipment dealer contracts and doesn't directly address jurisdictional clauses, the Law Explanation argues that limiting jurisdiction to a single definition may circumvent broader jurisdictional principles. The modification significantly weakens the original clause, potentially invalidating the entire jurisdiction clause, thus creating a legal contradiction under the 'Structural Flaws Legal Contradiction' definition provided, where the organization of the contract affects its enforceability.

perturbation.original_text: The obliga tions of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiati ons or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of 2 years from the date of this A greement.
perturbation.changed_text: The obligations of each Party concerning the color of the paper this document is printed on shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiations or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of 2 years from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By restricting the clause to the "color of the paper this document is printed on", the enforceability of the confidentiality obligations is compromised. This diminishes the agreement's protective scope for the parties' confidential information because the actual obligations are now tied to an irrelevant detail.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Limiting the duration clause to the color of paper undermines the 'reasonable efforts' requirement, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.

perturbation.justification: The modified text significantly weakens the protection of trade secrets, as it narrows the obligations to the color of the paper on which the document is printed, an irrelevant detail. The Law Explanation states that the Uniform Trade Secrets Act requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. By limiting the duration clause to the color of paper, the modified text undermines these 'reasonable efforts,' potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 2 describes the Uniform Trade Secrets Act as providing "states with a legal framework for improved trade secret protection to industry.". By limiting the obligations to the color of the paper, the new text creates a structural flaw that contradicts this legal framework, thereby creating a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This A greement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties and supersedes and extinguishes all previous drafts, agreements, arrangements and understandings between them,whether written or oral, relating to its subject matter.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the Parties regarding the proper method for stapling the document and supersedes and extinguishes all previous drafts, agreements, arrangements, and understandings between them, whether written or oral, relating to its subject matter.
perturbation.explanation: By specifying that the entire agreement only relates to the "proper method for stapling the document," this clause effectively nullifies the NDA's ability to protect confidential information. The entire agreement clause would prevent one party from arguing that separate agreements exist regarding confidential information, and limits it solely to stapling.
perturbation.law_explanation: This contradicts the general principles of Delaware contract law requiring a meeting of the minds and an intent to be bound by the contract's terms. By limiting the entire agreement clause to stapling, the parties did not show an intent to be bound to confidentiality.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a structural flaw that creates a legal contradiction. By limiting the entire agreement clause to the 'proper method for stapling the document,' it undermines the original intent of the clause, which was to cover the entire subject matter of the agreement. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that this limitation effectively nullifies the NDA's ability to protect confidential information, as the clause now only pertains to stapling methods. The Law Explanation states that this change contradicts the general principles of Delaware contract law, requiring a meeting of the minds and an intent to be bound by the contract's terms. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 is missing, and Scraped Law Snippet 2 is a LexisNexis blog post and not a legal code, the explanations provided outline the contradiction, and how the entire agreement clause is now limited, making the NDA unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: At the r equest of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) save in r elation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or whi ch is stored in electronic form; and (c) certi fy in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, orbased on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or anyapplicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipientto keep evidence that it has performed its obligations under this A greement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.changed_text: At the r equest of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information, but only if the document is written in Comic Sans font; (b) save in r elation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or whi ch is stored in electronic form; and (c) certi fy in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, orbased on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or anyapplicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipientto keep evidence that it has performed its obligations under this A greement. The provisions of this Agreement shall continue to apply to any documents and materials retained by the Recipient.
perturbation.explanation: By making the return/destruction contingent on the document being written in Comic Sans font, the obligation is rendered almost meaningless. Most confidential information will not be stored in this way, so there is no reasonable way to ensure compliance.
perturbation.law_explanation: UTSA requires 'efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances' to maintain secrecy. Making the destruction of confidential material conditional on the font used is not reasonable. It creates a loophole that defeats the act's primary intent.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: trade secret | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you trade secret The Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA) is a piece of legislation created by the Uniform Law Commission (ULC) . The UTSA defines trade secrets and describes claims related to trade secrets. As of 2024, 48 states , the District of Columbia, the U.S. Virgin Islands, and Puerto Rico, have adopted the UTSA. What is a Trade Secret UTSA's Definition The UTSA defines a " trade secret " as: Information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and Is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy. Non-UTSA Definition Prior to the development of the UTSA, improper use or disclosure of a trade secret was traditionally a common law tort . Sections 757 and 758 of the Restatement of Torts (1939) set forth the basic principles of trade secret law that were widely adopted by U.S. courts . In particular, §757, comment b, listed six factors to be considered in determining whether information constitutes a trade secret: The extent to which the information is known outside the claimant's business. The extent to which it is known by employees and others involved in the business. The extent of measures taken by the claimant to guard the secrecy of the information. The value of the information to the business and its competitors. The amount of effort or money expended by the business in developing the information. The ease or difficulty with which the information could be properly acquired or duplicated by others. Elements of a Trade Secret Claim There are typically three essential elements to a trade secret claim: The subject matter involved must qualify for trade secret protection (see "Scope" below for more on this). The holder of the subject matter must establish that reasonable precautions were taken to prevent disclosure of the subject matter. The trade secret holder must prove that the information was misappropriated or wrongfully taken. Use of a trade secret belonging to another does not always constitute misappropriation. There are two basic situations in which obtaining the use of a trade secret is illegal; where it is acquired through improper means, or where it involves a breach of confidence. Trade secrets may be obtained by lawful means such as independent discovery, reverse engineering, and inadvertent disclosure resulting from the trade secret holder's failure to take reasonable protective measures. The misappropriation of trade secrets is considered a form of unfair competition , and is discussed in the Restatement (Third) of Unfair Competition . Misappropriation of Trade Secrets as a Crime In some circumstances, misappropriation of trade secrets is not only a tort ; it is a federal crime . Economic Espionage Act In 1996, Congress enacted the Economic Espionage Act ("EEA"), codified as 18 U.S.C. §§1831-1839 . In United States v. Sazonov , the defendant Sazonov has been charged with violating the EEA for "attempt[ing] to steal valuable proprietary computer code that took his employer years to develop . . . Sazonov was employed for thirteen years by Susquehanna International Group ("Susquehanna"), a financial services firm headquartered in Pennsylvania with offices in Manhattan." Scope of Subject Matter Customer Lists Customer lists and other lists related to customer business qualify for trade secret protection if the lists' information cannot be ascertained from other generally available sources. In Morlife Inc. v. Perry, 56 Cal. App. 4th 1514 (1997) , the California Court held that customer identities from an organization's list are protected as trade secrets if the identities are not generally kno
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a condition (Comic Sans font) that undermines the reasonable efforts required to maintain secrecy of confidential information, as indicated by the UTSA. The original text requires the Recipient to destroy or return confidential information at the request of the disclosing party. The changed text adds a condition that the document must be in Comic Sans font to trigger the obligation. According to Scraped Law Snippet 1, the UTSA defines a trade secret as information that 'is the subject of efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy.' By limiting the destruction obligation to documents in Comic Sans, the changed text fails to provide 'efforts that are reasonable under the circumstances to maintain its secrecy,' because most confidential business documents are unlikely to be in Comic Sans.
